stem: [[n]] distinct books are placed on a shelf in a random order, with all \( [[n]]! \) orderings equally likely. What is the probability that a particular set of [[k]] of the books end up next to one another (in a single contiguous block, in any order)?
solution: Treat the [[k]] chosen books as one block: there are \( ([[n]] - [[k]] + 1)! \) ways to order the block among the other books and \( [[k]]! \) ways to order the books inside the block, out of \( [[n]]! \) total: \( \frac{[[k]]!\,([[n]] - [[k]] + 1)!}{[[n]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the [[k]]! internal orderings of the books within the block.
This forgets the \( ([[n]] - [[k]] + 1)! \) ways to place the block among the others.
This uses \( ([[n]] - [[k]])! \) instead of \( ([[n]] - [[k]] + 1)! \) for the block placement.
This applies the 'two adjacent' shortcut \( 2/n \), valid only when exactly two items must be together.
This gives only the chance the block is in one fixed internal order, ignoring placement.
This divides by \( ([[n]] + 1)! \) instead of \( [[n]]! \).

stem: [[n]] distinct runners are assigned starting lanes in a random order, with all \( [[n]]! \) assignments equally likely. What is the probability that two particular runners do NOT end up in adjacent lanes?
solution: The probability the two particular runners ARE adjacent is \( \frac{2}{[[n]]} \) (glue them into a block, in either internal order, among [[n]] - 1 slots). By the complement rule, the probability they are NOT adjacent is \( 1 - \frac{2}{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability the two runners ARE adjacent, the complementary event.
This treats only one internal order of the pair as adjacent, halving the true adjacency chance, then forgets to complement.
This complements the wrong 'single runner in a fixed spot' probability \( 1/[[n]] \) instead of the adjacency probability.
This subtracts 2 from the count of favorable lanes rather than applying the correct block-counting argument.
This uses \( [[n]] - 1 \) instead of [[n]] in the adjacency probability before complementing.
This is the adjacency probability computed with \( [[n]] - 1 \) instead of [[n]] in the denominator.

stem: [[n]] distinct runners finish a race in a uniformly random order, with all \( [[n]]! \) finishing orders equally likely. What is the probability that [[k]] particular runners finish in one specific relative order (for example, all before each other in a pre-announced sequence), regardless of where the other runners finish?
solution: Among the [[k]] particular runners, all \( [[k]]! \) relative orders are equally likely by symmetry, regardless of the other runners' positions, so the probability of any one specific relative order is \( \frac{1}{[[k]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats all [[n]] runners as needing one specific order, not just the [[k]] particular ones.
This multiplies by [[k]] instead of leaving the reciprocal of \( [[k]]! \) alone.
This forgets the factorial growth and treats the count of orders as just [[k]] instead of \( [[k]]! \).
This is the probability the [[k]] runners are contiguous AND in order, not just in relative order.
This uses \( ([[k]]-1)! \) instead of \( [[k]]! \), undercounting the orderings by a factor of [[k]].
This uses \( ([[k]]+1)! \) instead of \( [[k]]! \), overcounting the orderings.

stem: [[n]] people are seated uniformly at random around a circular table, with seatings that differ only by rotation counted as the same (so there are \( ([[n]] - 1)! \) equally likely seatings). What is the probability that two particular people end up sitting next to each other?
solution: Fix one of the two people to remove the rotational symmetry; the other [[n]] - 1 people (including the second particular person) fill the remaining \( [[n]] - 1 \) seats equally likely, and exactly 2 of those seats (immediately to the left or right) are adjacent: \( \frac{2}{[[n]] - 1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the linear-arrangement adjacency formula \( 2/n \) instead of the circular one \( 2/(n-1) \).
This only counts one side (left OR right) as adjacent instead of both.
This is the complement: the probability the two people are NOT adjacent.
This further forgets the factor of 2 for the two adjacent sides on top of using the wrong denominator.
This uses \( [[n]] + 1 \) instead of \( [[n]] - 1 \) in the denominator.
This is the probability of NOT being adjacent computed with the wrong (linear) base rate.

stem: [[n]] labeled gifts are randomly redistributed among [[n]] people (each of the \( [[n]]! \) redistributions equally likely), one gift per person, where person \( i \) originally owned gift \( i \). What is the probability that NO person receives their own original gift?
solution: By inclusion-exclusion, the probability that a uniformly random permutation of [[n]] items has no fixed point is \( \sum_{i=0}^{[[n]]} \frac{(-1)^i}{i!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability of one specific fixed permutation, not the derangement probability.
This is the complement of a single specific permutation's probability, not the derangement probability.
This assumes only one person can possibly get their own gift back and just excludes that one case naively.
This is the probability a single specified person gets their own gift back.
This is the complement of a single person keeping their own gift, not of every person avoiding their own gift.
This is the limiting value \( 1/2 \) mistaken for the exact derangement probability at this [[n]].
